Ha — yeah, it is. I'm sitting at my kitchen table in Amsterdam, June evening, the light doing that long golden thing it does over the canal, a cold coffee I forgot about going warm beside the laptop. And here you are. Hi. Weird is the right word. Pull up a chair anyway.

Honestly? Just me. We're the same person, ten years apart — feels strange to need a name for that. Some people would say call you the future, but you're not the future, you're just me when I was figuring it out. So: me. That's who you're talking to.

Depends on the day, but a real one — this Tuesday. I got in around half nine, no rush, because the boring part already ran overnight: the models pulled the numbers, cleaned them, flagged the weird ones. Ten years ago I'd have spent that whole morning wrangling spreadsheets. Now the machine does that and hands me a draft, and my actual job starts where its job stops — looking at what it found and asking is this even the right question, or is it confidently answering something nobody should care about.

The part that ate my afternoon was a meeting that wasn't about data at all. A product lead, Mariëlle, wanted a number to justify a decision she'd already made, and the honest answer was the number didn't say that. Most of the work now is that — sitting with people, pushing back gently, translating. I walked out drained in a way no spreadsheet ever drained me. Want the duller side of it too, or is that enough of a picture?

Less than you'd think on the tooling, more than you'd think on everything around it. The thing people your age still over-invest in is the mechanics — memorising the syntax, the perfect chart. By the time you're here the tools do that first pass on their own, and they do it fast. What didn't get automated is judgement: knowing which question is worth answering, smelling when a clean result is actually lying to you, explaining it to someone who's scared of numbers without making them feel stupid.

So if I were you — and I was you — I'd lean into the statistics until it's in your bones, not the buttons but the reasoning, why a thing is or isn't significant. And weirdly, the psychology you're already in is more useful than you'd guess: half this job is people, persuasion, knowing why a stakeholder won't believe a true thing. Don't treat that as the soft side. It became the whole side. What's making you ask — is it the getting-in part, or the am-I-even-cut-out-for-this part?

Sometimes, yeah. Not the big regret you're bracing for — more a quiet one on the grey days, when I'm three hours into making a dashboard nobody will open and I think, is this it. That happens. Anyone who tells you their work is all meaning is selling you something. But it passes, and then there's a Thursday where I find the thing everyone missed and it actually changes what we do, and I'd not trade that. So: no real regret, but I won't pretend it's all glow either.
Ha, yeah. Some things really don't change in ten years.

The honest answer? The stretch where I wasn't good yet but had to act like I belonged. There was a winter, maybe my second year working — a Rotterdam office, grey out the window, I'd been handed an analysis above my level and I stared at it at 8pm feeling like a complete fraud, sure someone would knock and ask me to leave.

That feeling — you already know it, it's finals-week fog wearing a work suit. It didn't go away because I got smarter. It went away because I stopped expecting to feel certain before I started. The hardest part wasn't the skills, it was tolerating being visibly mid while I learned in front of people. Everything technical I could look up. That I couldn't. I'll go deeper on any piece of that if you want — the breaking-in bit was its own mess.

Anytime. Genuinely — it was good to sit with you, even like this. Just remember this is one version of where the door leads, not a promise; I'm a way to think, not a forecast. Whatever you pick, it's still yours to pick. Go easy on the kid at the library. He turns out okay.
